                                                                                    CAUTION

                                      Periodically, the Bureau adjusts the industry employment series to a recent benchmark to improve
                                 their accuracy. These adjustments may also affect the hours and earnings series because employment
                                 levels are used as weights. Data from April 1965 forward are subject to revision at the time of the
                                 next benchmark adjustment.      Beginning with September 1966 and subsequent issues of Employment and
                                 Earnings and Monthly Report on the Labor Force, national data in Sections B, C, and D are based on
                                 March 1965 benchmarks. Comparable data for prior periods are published in Employment and Earnings
                                 Statistics for the United States, 1909-66, BLS Bulletin 1312-4.

                                      For further information regarding benchmark adjustments and other aspects of the program, the user
                                 is referred to the technical note at the back of this volume.




                    1
                        Quarterly data included in February, May, August, and November issues.

                                                                 Technical Note

                                                      Additional information concerning the preparation
                                                      of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                                      and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                                      survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                                      technical notes for each of these series, available
                                                      from the Buneau of Labor Statistics free of charge.


                   INTRODUCTION                                                    Relation between the household and payroll series

                  Reliability of the Estimates                                           The figures presented in table B are to be used for
                                                                                     other characteristics and are approximations of the
                       Since the estimates are based on a sample, they may           standard errors of all such characteristics. They should
                   differ from the figures that would have been obtained if          be interpreted as providing an indication of the order of
                   it were possible to take a complete census using the              magnitude of the standard errors rather than as the
                   same schedules and procedures.                                    precise standard error for any specific item.

                       The standard error is a measure of sampling varia-                The standard error of the change in an item from
                   bility, that is, the variations that might occur by chance        one month to the next month is more closely related to
                   because only a sample of the population is surveyed.              the standard error of the monthly level for that item than
                   The chances are about 2 out of 3 that an estimate from            to the size of the specific month-to-month change itself.
                   the sample would differ from a complete census by less            Thus, in order to use the approximations to the standard

                  graphic location.                                                  comparability and accuracy of reporting, since the

                  identical techniques at the national and State levels,             includes the 12th of the month. The labor turnover

                   Design

                       The sampling plan used in the current employment
                   statistics program is an optimum allocation design known                The BLS sample of establishment employment and
                   as ''sampling proportionate to average size of establish-           payrolls is the largest monthly sampling operation in the
                   ment." The universe of establishments is stratified first           field of social statistics. The table that follows shows
                   by industry and then within each industry by size of                the approximate proportion of total employment in each
                   establishment in terms of employment. For each industry             industry division covered by the group of establishments

                   class cells on the basis of average employment per                  individual industries within the division may vary from
                   establishment in each cell. In practice, this is equivalent         the proportions shown.
                   to distributing the predetermined total number of estab-
                   lishments required in the sample among the cells on the
                   basis of the ratio of employment in each cell to total                   Approximate size and coverage of BLS employment
